projects.name: LumaCV: AI Resume Intelligence
projects.description:
**Problem:** Resume tailoring is a manual, error-prone process that often fails ATS screening due to poor keyword alignment and inconsistent formatting. 
**Solution:** Built an AI-first resume intelligence platform using multi-LLM orchestration to parse resumes, analyze job descriptions, and generate tailored, ATS-optimized resumes with deterministic LaTeX rendering. 
**Impact:** Reduced resume tailoring time from ~30 minutes to under 60 seconds while ensuring consistent formatting, improved keyword coverage, and higher ATS compatibility.
projects.summary: AI-powered resume intelligence platform that automates ATS-optimized resume generation using multi-LLM failover and deterministic LaTeX rendering.

detail.overview: LumaCV is a full-stack AI system designed to automate resume tailoring at scale. It combines LLM-based parsing, semantic job description analysis, and deterministic LaTeX rendering to produce production-grade resumes with minimal user input. The system is built with reliability in mind, incorporating model failover, rate limiting, and state persistence.
stackHighlights: Next.js App Router with Server Actions
Upstash-based rate limiting and request control

architecture.title: Resume Builder Client
architecture.detail: Multi-step wizard (Upload PDF + JD → Edit Details → AI Processing → Preview + Score) with persistent client state.

architecture.title: AI Orchestration Layer
architecture.detail: Multi-provider failover across GPT-4o Mini, Llama 3.3, Gemini Flash, and Claude 3.5 so degraded vendors don't block resume generation.

architecture.title: PDF Compilation
architecture.detail: Serverless-compatible PDF rendering—Vercel Hobby tier can't fit LaTeX engines, so compilation is offloaded to external REST API.

detail.architectureMermaid:
flowchart LR
  subgraph Input
    U[User Resume / JD]
  end
  subgraph AI
    P[LLM Parser]
    A[JD Analyzer]
    T[Tailoring Engine]
  end
  subgraph Infra
    S[(Supabase Auth)]
    Z[Zustand State]
    R[Rate Limiter]
  end
  subgraph Output
    L[LaTeX Engine]
    PDF[Final Resume]
  end
  U --> P --> A --> T
  T --> L --> PDF
  S <--> T
  Z <--> U
  R --> T

decisions.title: External LaTeX service over local compilation
decisions.context: Vercel Hobby tier can't fit LaTeX engines due to size constraints
decisions.decision: Offload PDF compilation to external REST API to stay on free tier without sacrificing output quality

decisions.title: Multi-LLM failover
decisions.context: Single provider downtime blocks users and creates bad experience
decisions.decision: OpenRouter routing with automatic fallback across 4 providers (GPT-4o Mini, Llama, Gemini, Claude)

decisions.title: Client-side PDF parsing
decisions.context: Serverless functions have payload limits and adding file upload handling increases complexity
decisions.decision: Use pdfjs-dist in browser to extract text before sending to APIs, keeping server routes lightweight

pipeline.detail: Auto-deploy from GitHub, Next.js detected and optimized
Prevent API abuse on LLM endpoints with Redis-backed rate limiting
maxDuration: 60 for LLM routes, Edge runtime for scoring endpoints

incidents.title: Vercel 10s function timeout on large resumes
incidents.resolution: Increased maxDuration to 60s and implemented client-side PDF parsing to reduce payload size
incidents.lesson: Serverless timeouts are hard limits—offload heavy work to client or external services

incidents.title: LaTeX compiler rendering inconsistencies
incidents.resolution: Standardized on single external LaTeX service with deterministic templates
incidents.lesson: Browser PDF generation is inconsistent—deterministic LaTeX pipelines ensure predictable output

incidents.title: Live editing box performance issues
incidents.resolution: Debounced input handling and virtualized large text areas for smooth real-time preview
incidents.lesson: Live preview features need debouncing and virtualization to handle large documents smoothly

projects.name: LinkedIn Content Automation Pipeline
projects.description:
**Problem:** LinkedIn content creation is time-intensive, repetitive, and often lacks real-time relevance, reducing posting consistency. 
**Solution:** Built an AI-driven n8n automation pipeline with live research, LLM-based generation, and Discord-triggered workflows with Supabase state management. 
**Impact:** Automated end-to-end ideation to publishing, reducing effort from hours to seconds and enabling consistent, high-quality output.
projects.summary: AI-assisted LinkedIn content pipeline that turns Discord prompts into researched, deduped posts in seconds instead of hours.

detail.overview: End-to-end automation from research and ideation to drafted posts and one-click publish, with human-in-the-loop via Discord and durable state for deduplication.
detail.problem: LinkedIn content creation was manual and inconsistent. Hours spent on research, drafting, and scheduling with no deduplication — leading to repeated topics and missed posting windows.
detail.solution: Built an n8n automation pipeline on AWS EC2 with Discord as the trigger interface. LLM-powered research and drafting with Supabase for stateful deduplication. Google Drive for asset management, LinkedIn API for direct publishing.
detail.tradeoffs: Dependency on multiple external APIs (Discord, LinkedIn, Google Drive) creates fragility. LLM output quality varies — requires human-in-the-loop review. Self-hosted n8n on EC2 means managing infra for a content tool.
results: Hours to seconds per content cycle
Zero duplicate topics via Supabase state tracking
Consistent posting cadence with minimal manual effort

architecture.title: AI Content Engine
architecture.detail: Three interconnected workflows: daily AI-powered idea pitcher with live web research and deduplication, content creator triggered by Discord that drafts posts and generates images, and one-click publisher that posts to LinkedIn.

architecture.title: State Management
architecture.detail: Stores idea deduplication state, user preferences, and tracks which ideas have been pitched—prevents topic repetition across campaigns.

architecture.title: Control Interface
architecture.detail: Sole control interface for the entire pipeline interactive buttons for approve/reject/publish, no manual LinkedIn interaction required.

beforeAfter.label: Ideation
beforeAfter.after: Deduped AI-assisted ideas delivered on schedule

detail.architectureMermaid:
flowchart LR
  subgraph ingest [Trigger & control]
    D[Discord]
  end
  subgraph compute [Automation]
    N[n8n on EC2]
    L[LLM + research]
  end
  subgraph data [State & assets]
    S[(Supabase)]
    G[Google Drive]
  end
  subgraph publish [Outbound]
    LI[LinkedIn API]
  end
  D --> N
  N --> L
  N --> S
  N --> G
  N --> LI
  S --> N

decisions.title: n8n over custom code
decisions.context: Needed rapid workflow iteration without deployments for a content automation tool
decisions.decision: Chose n8n visual workflow builder for AI-powered pipelines—allowed iterating on LLM prompts and API integrations without code changes

decisions.title: Discord as control plane
decisions.context: Team already used Discord for daily communication
decisions.decision: Made Discord the single interface for triggering, reviewing, and publishing—no separate dashboard needed, minimal context switching

pipeline.detail: Interactive buttons for /generate-ideas action
Live web research and idea generation with deduplication
AI-powered post drafting with image generation
Interactive buttons for select ideas with dropdown action
Interactive buttons for approve/reject actions
3 interconnected workflows handle ideation → drafting → publishing with LLM reasoning
State management and deduplication in Supabase, AI-generated images stored in Drive
One-click automated posting after Discord approval

incidents.title: Free tier API limits causing workflow failures
incidents.resolution: Implemented multi-AI fallback mechanism and concise prompt engineering to reduce token usage
incidents.lesson: Free tiers have hard limits—design fallback chains and optimize prompts for cost efficiency

incidents.title: Discord bot webhook connectivity issues
incidents.resolution: Configured n8n webhook node with retry logic and proper Discord bot token management
incidents.lesson: Webhook integrations need retry logic and proper authentication handling

incidents.title: Web search API rate limiting
incidents.resolution: Cached research results and batched searches to minimize API calls
incidents.lesson: Research APIs are expensive—cache aggressively and batch requests

projects.name: Multi-Tenant Merchant Platform
projects.description:
**Problem:** FTP ingress was a bottleneck with frequent downtime, costing clients money.
**Solution:** Built a 99.99% available multi-tenant SFTP gateway with automated AWS provisioning.
**Impact:** Zero unplanned downtime; supports 500+ merchants simultaneously.
projects.summary: Highly available SFTP ingress on AWS that onboards hundreds of merchants without sacrificing reliability.

detail.overview: Multi-tenant SFTP ingress with automated provisioning and strong isolation so merchant onboarding doesn't compromise reliability.
detail.problem: FTP ingress was a single point of failure. Frequent downtime during peak merchant onboarding. AWS costs inflated by NAT Gateway overuse and unoptimized data transfer paths.
detail.solution: Built a multi-tenant SFTP gateway with automated AWS provisioning via Terraform. Replaced NAT Gateway with VPC endpoints for S3 traffic. Implemented connection pooling and tenant isolation at the network level.
detail.tradeoffs: Higher operational complexity from multi-tenant isolation. Terraform state management overhead. Chose availability over simplicity — accepted more moving parts for 99.99% uptime.

architecture.title: FTP Gateway
architecture.detail: Self-managed SFTP on EC2 with chroot jail isolation—merchants and suppliers connect to shared infrastructure with strict boundaries.

architecture.title: Tenant Isolation
architecture.detail: Linux-level chroot jails enforce directory boundaries—merchants cannot escape their dedicated folders, access controlled at OS level.

architecture.title: Credential Management
architecture.detail: 1 supplier can have multiple merchants. Only suppliers can create passwords and users. Custom feed creation generates user/password pairs auto-assigned to respective tenant directories, shareable with specific suppliers.

beforeAfter.label: Ingress reliability
beforeAfter.after: Standardized SFTP gateway with automated fleet patterns

detail.architectureMermaid:
flowchart TB
  M[Merchants] --> SFTP[SFTP gateway tier]
  SFTP --> S3[(Object storage)]
  SFTP --> MET[Metadata & routing]
  MET --> AUTO[Terraform-managed infra]

decisions.title: EC2 over managed SFTP
decisions.context: AWS Transfer Family was expensive for multi-tenant use case with many users
decisions.decision: Self-managed SFTP on EC2 with custom user isolation logic using chroot—significantly lower cost for high user count

decisions.title: chroot over containerization
decisions.context: Needed lightweight isolation without Docker overhead on a single EC2 instance
decisions.decision: Linux-level chroot jails with chmod for directory restrictions—simpler, lower resource footprint than containers

pipeline.detail: Auto-create user, password, and chroot environment per tenant with proper permissions
Single instance serving all tenants with resource monitoring and SSH access for management
chmod/chroot enforce boundaries at OS level—merchants cannot access other directories

incidents.title: API endpoints exposed without authentication
incidents.resolution: Implemented private API authentication and integrated with backend for proper access control
incidents.lesson: All APIs must be authenticated by default—never assume internal-only access is safe

incidents.title: Backend integration breaking on user creation
incidents.resolution: Standardized API contracts and added validation for supplier-merchant relationship creation
incidents.lesson: Multi-tenant systems need strict relationship validation between entities

projects.name: Real-Time Data Pipeline
projects.description:
**Problem:** Analytics queries took minutes to return, blocking decision-making.
**Solution:** Architected a real-time ELT pipeline using Dagster, DLT, and ClickHouse.
**Impact:** Query latency reduced from 5–10 minutes to under 100ms, enabling real-time dashboards.
projects.summary: Real-time ELT stack with Dagster and ClickHouse that turns minute-long analytics waits into sub-100ms dashboards.

detail.overview: From batch pain to near-real-time analytics by orchestrating assets in Dagster and serving ClickHouse for interactive use.

architecture.title: Extract Layer
architecture.detail: Sling pulls MySQL tables incrementally every 6 hours (only new/changed rows), DLT pulls MongoDB collections (full sync).

architecture.title: Orchestration
architecture.detail: Manages job dependencies, schedules, and asset-based lineage—runs in serverless mode with proper dependency graphs.

architecture.title: Transform
architecture.detail: Creates denormalized views (orders_data, product_data) with business logic and data quality rules applied after raw loads.

architecture.title: Warehouse + Consumption
architecture.detail: Raw data lands in ClickHouse (tables prefixed: buyogo_mysql_*, dlt_new_mongo_*), business users query via Metabase dashboards.

detail.architectureMermaid:
flowchart LR
  SRC[Sources] --> DLT[DLT ingest]
  DLT --> CH[(ClickHouse)]
  D[Dagster orchestration] --> DLT
  D --> CH
  CH --> BI[Dashboards]

decisions.title: Sling for MySQL, DLT for MongoDB
decisions.context: Different data patterns—MySQL needed incremental sync, MongoDB full replace
decisions.decision: Best-of-breed tools per source rather than one-size-fits-all: Sling for incremental MySQL, DLT for MongoDB collections

decisions.title: Dagster over Airflow
decisions.context: Needed modern orchestration with asset-based lineage and better observability
decisions.decision: Dagster Cloud for serverless deployment—explicit dependency graphs and software-defined assets

decisions.title: ClickHouse over PostgreSQL
decisions.context: Analytical queries on large datasets were slow in row-based stores
decisions.decision: Columnar ClickHouse Cloud for 10x query performance—sub-100ms dashboards instead of 5-10 minute waits

pipeline.detail: MySQL incremental every 6h via Sling, MongoDB full sync via DLT
Raw data lands in prefixed tables: buyogo_mysql_*, dlt_new_mongo_*
Creates orders_data and product_data views with business logic
Manages dependencies, retries, scheduling via GitHub Actions deployment
Branch-based: main→prod, staging→staging, test→testing environments
Dashboards for processed data with better visuals

incidents.title: DBT temp table conflicts in shared ClickHouse
incidents.resolution: Added cleanup query to pipeline—DBT temp tables now cleaned between runs
incidents.lesson: Shared ClickHouse schema needs explicit cleanup between runs; don't assume automatic cleanup

projects.name: Infrastructure as Code Migration
projects.description:
**Problem:** Manual infrastructure changes led to drift, inconsistency, and slow recovery. 
**Solution:** Led comprehensive ClickOps→Terraform migration for all AWS assets with full audit trail. 
**Impact:** MTTR down 40%, infrastructure versioned, repeatability guaranteed.
projects.summary: End-to-end AWS ClickOps to Terraform migration that cut MTTR by 40% and made every infra change reviewable.

detail.overview: Replaced ad-hoc console changes with Terraform-managed infrastructure and reviewable workflows so recovery and scaling follow predictable patterns.

architecture.title: State Management
architecture.detail: Remote state with workspace isolation per environment—state locking prevents concurrent modifications.

architecture.title: Legacy Migration
architecture.detail: Imported existing CloudFormation stacks to Terraform using terraform import workflows—gradual, not big-bang.

architecture.title: Validation
architecture.detail: terraform plan and custom policy checks required before any apply reaches production.

decisions.title: Terraform over CloudFormation
decisions.context: ClickOps was error-prone and unreviewable—console changes had no audit trail
decisions.decision: Terraform for version-controlled, reviewable infrastructure—all changes go through PR review

decisions.title: Gradual migration over big-bang
decisions.context: Critical services couldn't have downtime during migration
decisions.decision: Import existing resources incrementally using terraform import rather than recreating infrastructure

decisions.title: Terraform Cloud over local state
decisions.context: Team needed collaboration and state locking for concurrent changes
decisions.decision: Remote state with CI/CD integration—prevents state conflicts and enables team-wide visibility

pipeline.detail: terraform fmt, validate, and plan on every PR
Infrastructure changes require human approval before production apply
Automated apply after merge with state locking and audit trail

incidents.title: Generic resource names causing import conflicts
incidents.resolution: Established naming conventions with environment prefixes and resource types before importing
incidents.lesson: Use descriptive, consistent naming schemes—generic names like 'instance-1' cause confusion and import errors

incidents.title: Terraform import state drift
incidents.resolution: Imported existing CloudFormation resources with terraform import and immediately ran terraform plan to reconcile state
incidents.lesson: Import then immediately plan to catch drift between actual infrastructure and desired state

projects.name: CI/CD Pipeline Optimization
projects.description:
**Problem:** Deployments were slow and deployment confidence was low due to serial testing. 
**Solution:** Redesigned GitHub Actions with semantic versioning, parallelized test suites, and intelligent caching. 
**Impact:** 30% faster deployments, increased release frequency from 1x/week to daily.
projects.summary: Rebuilt GitHub Actions pipelines to ship daily with ~30% faster, better-cached deployments.

detail.overview: Engineering velocity through parallel jobs, better caching, and release discipline—not by skipping quality gates.

architecture.title: Workflow Engine
architecture.detail: Parallel job execution with matrix—unit tests, smoke tests, and Terraform validation run simultaneously instead of sequentially.

architecture.title: Quality Gates
architecture.detail: Unit tests → smoke tests → Terraform plan/validation → PR merge gate. All must pass before merge is allowed.

architecture.title: Artifact Optimization
architecture.detail: Shared build artifacts across jobs eliminate redundant downloads—cache dependencies once, reuse in all downstream jobs.

architecture.title: Branch Protection
architecture.detail: PR must pass all checks (tests, Terraform validation, smoke tests) before merge to main is permitted—devs cannot bypass.

decisions.title: Matrix strategy over sequential
decisions.context: Pipeline was taking too long with serialized test phases—mostly waiting, not computing
decisions.decision: Parallel job execution with matrix for faster feedback—unit tests, smoke tests, and Terraform validation run together

decisions.title: PR-blocking gates
decisions.context: Broken code was merging to main and causing production issues
decisions.decision: Required checks (tests, Terraform validation, smoke tests) must pass before merge allowed—no exceptions

decisions.title: Artifact caching
decisions.context: Re-downloading dependencies on every job was slow and wasteful
decisions.decision: Shared artifact storage between workflow steps—cache once, reuse everywhere, significantly reduced PR-to-merge time

pipeline.detail: Matrix parallel execution, artifact caching for dependencies
Unit tests + smoke tests + Terraform unit tests + Terraform plan/validation
All checks must pass—no merge until tests, Terraform validation, and smoke tests complete
Test results published for visibility and historical tracking

incidents.title: Long PR-to-merge time blocking velocity
incidents.resolution: Matrix parallelization and artifact caching reduced time—sequential pipelines don't scale
incidents.lesson: Sequential pipelines become bottlenecks as team grows—parallelize early and cache aggressively

incidents.title: Artifacts being re-downloaded on every job
incidents.resolution: Implemented shared artifact storage between workflow steps—dependencies cached once and reused
incidents.lesson: Cache at the right granularity—per-workflow caches beat per-job downloads

incidents.title: Matrix jobs not waiting for completion
incidents.resolution: Configured needs dependency to ensure all matrix jobs complete before starting dependent stages
incidents.lesson: Explicitly declare job dependencies—parallel jobs need coordination points

projects.name: Observability & Monitoring Platform
projects.description:
**Problem:** Incidents took hours to diagnose due to fragmented monitoring. 
**Solution:** Implemented service-level observability with Prometheus and Grafana, unified SLI tracking. 
**Impact:** MTTD reduced by 60%, on-call burden decreased significantly.
projects.summary: Service-level observability platform with Prometheus and Grafana that shrinks incident detection time by 60%.

detail.overview: Service-level golden signals and dashboards that match how engineers actually triage—not vanity charts.

architecture.title: Metrics Collection
architecture.detail: Scrapes service metrics, golden signals, and deployment health indicators from applications and infrastructure.

architecture.title: Visualization
architecture.detail: Service-level views tied to SLOs—latency, traffic, errors, saturation—for faster triage and diagnosis.

architecture.title: Log Aggregation
architecture.detail: Centralized logging for AWS services and application logs—correlates with metrics for root cause analysis.

architecture.title: Alert Routing
architecture.detail: Multi-channel alerts: Teams/Slack for standard alerts, Email for critical—routes based on severity with context.

detail.architectureMermaid:
flowchart TB
  SRV[Services] --> PROM[Prometheus]
  PROM --> GRAF[Grafana]
  PROM --> AM[Alertmanager]
  AM --> ON[On-call]

decisions.title: Prometheus + Grafana over CloudWatch alone
decisions.context: CloudWatch dashboards were limited and expensive for custom metrics
decisions.decision: Self-managed observability stack with better visualization and lower cost for high-cardinality metrics

decisions.title: Tiered alerting
decisions.context: Alert fatigue from non-critical notifications going to same channel as incidents
decisions.decision: Route standard alerts to Teams/Slack, critical alerts to Email—context-aware routing reduces noise

pipeline.detail: Metric scraping from services and infrastructure into time-series database
Time-series data storage with retention policies for historical analysis
Dashboards and exploration for latency, traffic, errors, saturation metrics
Multi-channel routing based on severity—Teams/Slack for standard, Email for critical

incidents.title: Prometheus scrape targets flapping
incidents.resolution: Increased scrape timeout and implemented service discovery health checks
incidents.lesson: Default timeouts don't work for all services—tune scrape intervals per target

incidents.title: Grafana dashboard permissions too broad
incidents.resolution: Implemented role-based access control with team-specific folder permissions
incidents.lesson: Dashboard access should follow least privilege—not everyone needs edit access

incidents.title: Alertmanager routing misconfiguration
incidents.resolution: Fixed routing tree configuration to ensure critical alerts reach email while standard alerts go to Slack
incidents.lesson: Test alert routing with simulated alerts—misconfigurations silence critical notifications

projects.name: Auth0 Tenant Migration
projects.description:
**Problem:** Legacy Auth0 tenant had security and scalability issues. 
**Solution:** Executed zero-downtime migration for 1,000+ users with least-privilege IAM via AWS SSO. 
**Impact:** Zero user disruption, improved security posture, unified identity system.
projects.summary: Zero-downtime Auth0 migration for 1,000+ users, aligned with least-privilege IAM and AWS SSO.

detail.overview: Identity migration with rollback strategy and IAM alignment so security improvements don't become a customer-facing outage.

architecture.title: Source Systems
architecture.detail: Legacy customers on Lynks Auth0 tenant, new customers on Rivafy Auth0. Hidden AWS Cognito layer discovered during migration was proxying requests and adding cost.

architecture.title: Migration Coordination
architecture.detail: Direct calls with Auth0 executives to obtain password hash export,required board-level approval due to sensitive credential data.

architecture.title: Target System
architecture.detail: Seamless user migration without password reset prompts—users never experienced disruption or incorrect password messages.

architecture.title: Cost Optimization
architecture.detail: Discovered Auth0 was fetching from Cognito for legacy users—migrated Cognito users directly to Auth0, eliminating $15k/year intermediate layer.

decisions.title: Password hash export over force-reset
decisions.context: Forcing password resets would damage brand reputation and create support burden—users would see 'incorrect password' errors
decisions.decision: Worked with Auth0 executives to obtain password hashes for seamless migration—no user disruption, no password reset prompts

decisions.title: Board-level escalation
decisions.context: Password hash export requires executive approval due to sensitive credential data handling
decisions.decision: Engaged board members to secure necessary permissions—owned end-to-end from discovery through execution

decisions.title: Post-migration architecture review
decisions.context: After Auth0 migration, noticed unexplained AWS Cognito API calls in billing
decisions.decision: Deep dive revealed Auth0 was proxying to Cognito for legacy users—migrated them directly to Auth0, cut $15k/year

pipeline.detail: Identified split tenant situation—legacy users on Lynks Auth0, new users on Rivafy Auth0
Secured password hash export approval—required board-level sign-off for sensitive credential data
User data migration without password reset prompts—seamless auth continuity
Discovered hidden Cognito costs, migrated legacy Cognito users—saved $15k/year alone

incidents.title: Split tenant discovery during rebranding
incidents.resolution: End-to-end ownership including Auth0 executive calls and board escalation—password hash export approved and executed
incidents.lesson: User-facing auth changes require executive-level coordination to avoid brand damage—don't force password resets on rebrand

incidents.title: Hidden Cognito proxy costs
incidents.resolution: Migrated legacy Cognito users directly to Auth0, eliminated intermediate layer
incidents.lesson: $15k/year savings from post-migration architecture review—always audit costs after major infrastructure changes
